focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Her chest caught before there was enough light to call it morning. Hester turned from the dough trough and coughed into her apron. The cough dragged from deep enough to make her eyes water. When she wiped her mouth, the spit on the cloth held a faint gray in it. It was dust again. It was always dust. It sat in the folds of her throat the way old bitterness sat in a family, never leaving cleanly, only thinning and returning. If she was going to behave like a fool, she would at least do it cleanly. Six washed fruit jars stood on her back table beside a bent teaspoon and a square of muslin she had boiled the night before.

The bakery still held its night smells. Yeast had gone slack in the sponge crock. Coffee grounds sat damp in the pot from yesterday. Flour lived in the boards. Wood smoke cooled in the iron throat of the range. Under all of it sat the storm, the same way it sat under everything now, as if Black Sunday had taken a room in every house in Morrow and did not mean to pay. Dust lay on the sill above the dough trough, in the groove of the cake case glass, on the enamel breadbox, and over the little mirror by the washstand in the curtained room where she slept. She had wiped once already since the storm and there was still enough grit left to write a lie in.

Yesterday, leaning across the cake case to see whether the front pane had gone permanently blind, she had breathed on the glass and seen Otto's hand there for one blink: not his face, not enough for comfort, only his hand setting two survey stakes against a wall. The hand had gone before she could even say his name. That could have been grief. She had been living with grief long enough to know it could dress itself in plain clothes and pass for ordinary sight if you let it. After Marta died, th...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e she had lived inside since the barn, a lie so clean it had almost counted as mercy.

"Nothing," she said, because the word arrived first and because she did not yet know what name the other thing would bear.

Thea stood in the doorway another second, searching her face and getting nowhere with it. Then Dell coughed in his sleep from the settle, and that broke the moment cleanly enough for both of them. Thea did not ask again. After a while her tread crossed the upstairs boards and went still.

Cora stayed in the front room with the lamp and the window. She wiped one hard oval in the glass until the rag turned to mud in her hand and the rest of the pane still wore the storm. Each time she breathed close, she expected August's office to come back through the clouding and show her some fresh treachery. It never did. There was only her own face and the dark porch beyond it and the grit settling deeper into the house. Once she told herself plainly that she had imagined it. Then she saw again, with sickening exactness, the green worked into Ezra's split nail, and the lie would not go back where it had been.

Near dawn she carried the rag out to the pump and worked the handle in the dark, listening to the iron complain. Brown water ran over her wrists and down into the packed yard. It did not wash the sight of Ezra signing from her. Nothing was finished. Nothing was buried. The storm had left dust in the seams of the house and something worse in her hands: not proof fit to carry into town, not yet, but a thing she could not unknow. Ezra had taken a pen from August Poeschel, or else Cora had invented the one detail she had already scrubbed from his flesh with her own hands. Either way the old story was done. Whatever the weather had not stripped from her that day, this would.
